rationale: Bottomley and Holden (2001) is used in the citing paper's theoretical framing, not just in a generic literature list. The authors cite it both to support the core quality-and-fit basis of extension evaluations and, more specifically, to support the claim that parent-brand quality can directly drive evaluations even when fit is weaker. That role is central to motivating the paper's main question about when consumers weight quality versus fit more heavily in different decision contexts.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Bottomley and Holden (2001) as key prior evidence that parent-brand quality has a direct effect in brand extension evaluation, alongside fit. It builds its conceptual setup on that foundation to examine contextual conditions that shift the relative weight of quality versus fit.
